sections.title: Changes Around Us
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens with a narrative of two friends, Saba and Aparna, noticing changes in each other after a year apart — height, hairstyle, new skills — and discussing how things around them have changed too. Students are introduced to the idea that change is constant and happens at different timescales: some changes take years, some happen daily, and some occur in minutes or seconds. Examples include growing taller each year, leaves falling and regrowing, flowers blooming and drying, the sky changing colour, and the sun appearing to rise and set.

tasks.task_text: Try to remember the time you were in Grades 3 and 4. Think and write about the changes that you have noticed in yourself and your friends, in your school, and in your environment.
Can you think of something that takes a really long time to change, maybe over years?
What about something that changes within just a few years?
Are there changes that happen every year, again and again?
Can you name something that changes in just a few minutes or even seconds?

sections.title: Changes Around Us in a Day (Day and Night)
sections.content_summary: This section explains the cause of day and night through the Earth's rotation. Students first enact a physical role-play game where one student plays the Sun and another plays Earth, rotating to experience day and night. A torch-and-globe demonstration is then used to show how the Earth rotating on its axis — not the Sun moving — creates day and night. Students learn that the Sun stays fixed while different parts of the Earth face the light as it rotates. A 'Do you know?' box explains what a globe is, that seas and oceans cover three-fourths of the Earth, and the difference between seas and oceans.

tasks.task_text: Let one student be the 'Sun' and another the 'Earth' standing at a distance. The Sun stands at the centre. Let the student playing 'Earth' stand in one place and slowly turn around (rotate). As long as the student sees the 'Sun', they keep saying—'day…day…day', looking straight ahead. When the student stops seeing the 'Sun' they keep saying 'night…night…night'. Now, let us do this activity using a torch and a globe.
Study the globe and write in the space given below, when it is day time in India, which countries will have night?

sections.title: Changes Around Us in a Year (Seasons)
sections.content_summary: This section uses Saba and Aparna's Seasons' Journal as a launch point for a sustained classroom activity where students compile their year-long nature observations. A four-step Classroom Activity — Seasons' Chart asks students to build a wall chart mapping five themes (plant life, birds and animals, air/heat/light, water and water bodies, human activities) across four time periods (April–June, July–September, October–December, January–March). Students fill the chart from their journals, form theme groups to make posters, then regroup by time period to write short stories linking nature changes to human life. Through this synthesis, students discover that these yearly patterns repeat — and these repeating patterns are called seasons. India's six seasons are introduced using their Sanskrit names: Vasanta, Grishma, Varsha, Sharad, Hemant, Shishir, with a table for students to fill in the months for their own region. The IKS note that in Odisha, the koel bird's song signals the rains and farmers begin planting is presented in the margin.

tasks.task_text: Classroom Activity — Seasons' Chart: Step 1 — Prepare a large wall-sized chart with time periods (April–June, July–September, October–December, January–March) as columns and themes (plant life, birds and animals, air/heat/light, water and water bodies, human activities) as rows. Step 2 — Fill the chart from your journal observations by sharing across the class. Step 3 — Form five groups by theme, create a poster showing how your theme changed throughout the year, and present to the class. Step 4 — Form four new groups by time period; analyse all five themes for your time period and create a short story linking nature with people's lives.

sections.title: Celebrating Seasons
sections.content_summary: This section connects seasons to India's cultural and agricultural life. Students find out songs associated with each season and learn that different crops grow in different seasons — some need winter cold, some summer heat, some monsoon rain. A table tasks students with naming summer and winter crops in their region with the help of teachers and elders. Indian festivals are explicitly linked to seasons: Pongal/Makar Sankranti (winter), Holi (spring), Diwali (autumn), and Baisakhi/Gudi Padwa/Vishu/Rongali Bihu (new year/harvest). The IKS note that ants carrying eggs to higher ground signals approaching rain is in the margin. A 'Let us reflect' section closes the chapter with reflective writing and drawing tasks on personal season experiences, regional season names in local languages, season-linked food/clothes/festivals, seasons journaling, and comparative drawing.

tasks.task_text: How do you feel when the season changes? Would you prefer one season to continue throughout the year? Give the reasons for your answer.
Winter in Kerala is different from winter in Kashmir valley. Monsoon in Assam is different from Rajasthan. Why do you think it is so?
Narrate your experiences about the season changes.
Ask your family members and write about seasons. Do you find any major differences in the patterns or changing seasons? What could be possible reasons for these differences?

Seasons have local names. Find out and write the seasons mentioned below are called in your region, and your local language. (a) Spring: (b) Summer: (c) Monsoon: (d) Autumn: (e) Pre-winter: (f) Winter:

Fill the table below based on your observations and experiences. (Name of the Season | Food You Eat | Clothes You Wear | Festivals You Celebrate | Nature Around You — for Spring, Summer, Monsoon, Autumn, Pre-winter, Winter)
Share your experience of seasons' journaling.
Make a comparative drawing of the three major seasons. (Summer | Monsoon | Winters)

dual_strand.natural: Earth's rotation causing day and night; seasonal cycles as repeating annual patterns driven by Earth's movement
dual_strand.human_cultural: India's six-season (Ritu) system, seasonal festivals, crops, songs, regional diversity of seasons, and traditional ecological knowledge (koel bird, ant behaviour)
